name: Lew Soloff
abstract: Lewis Michael Soloff (February 20, 1944 – March 8, 2015) was an American jazz trumpeter, composer, and actor. He was a member of the band Blood, Sweat & Tears.

interviews.title: Lew Soloff interviewed by Monk Rowe, Scottsdale, Arizona, April 16, 2000

quotes.text: Yeah my conscious reason for picking up the trumpet was because it was shiny and the odd thing is that I — I don’t know how you would put it — but I wound up liking a dull, straight brass-looking horn. As my horn got dull and brass I liked it. Now I also have a beautiful, shiny gold trumpet that I like. I have two trumpets, both of them Bach trumpets, one an old one made in New York City in 1947. I got it second-hand about I’d say close to 20 years ago I got it. And it’s a magnificent instrument, but it doesn’t have a lot of lead trumpet projection anymore. It kind of mellowed and got darker and richer, but lost the lead trumpet projection. So I found a trumpet that has amazing lead trumpet projection and also plays very well — if I didn’t have this other one I’d probably be playing it for everything. But I use that when I play strong lead, like with the Carnegie Hall Jazz Band or something like that. And it’s just beautiful and it’s shiny. But I was walking home from college one day, back to the dorm, and all of a sudden I started to sing to myself Roy Eldridge’s solo from “After You’ve Gone.” And I sung it note for note as I was walking home from school and I realized that I hadn’t heard it since I was five years old. And I said wait a minute. I knew that solo cold from when I was five years old. And then I sung a Louis Armstrong solo or two that I knew from when I was five years old. And I said that’s why I took up the trumpet. That’s why I took up the trumpet. And I was very fortunate that my grandmother and grandfather and my uncle liked those records, because that’s what drove me into jazz and that’s what drove me into playing trumpet. I listened to Roy Eldridge and Louis Armstrong when I was a little kid. How much luckier can you be?
quotes.caption: Mentioning playing with the Carnegie Hall Jazz Band
quotes.doc_title: Lew Soloff interviewed by Monk Rowe, Scottsdale, Arizona, April 16, 2000

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Malone
about_quotes.evidence: I was a trumpet player with Blood, Sweat & Tears with Lew Soloff, 1973.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tom Malone interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 3, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Marty Sheller
about_quotes.evidence: An old friend. A great trumpet player... He recorded a couple of things that I composed and a really good friend.
about_quotes.doc_title: Marty Sheller interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 9, 2022

about_quotes.speaker: Gap Mangione
about_quotes.evidence: That band consisted of Snooky Young, Lew Soloff, Jon Faddis. That was the trumpet section
about_quotes.doc_title: Gap Mangione interviewed by Monk Rowe, Rochester, New York, January 11, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Loren Schoenberg
about_quotes.evidence: Also studying with him at that time were Jon Faddis and Lew Soloff... And I remember they were crazy and I remember meeting other people in those days at his studio.
about_quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: I was just going to say Lew Soloff. Let’s see. Who else was in there? ... We had Victor Sproles on bass. Don Friedman was there for a while
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jerry Dodgion
about_quotes.evidence: The trumpet section of that band is always great because they have Lew Soloff and Earl Gardner and Byron Stripling and Randy Brecker.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jerry Dodgion interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, March 9, 1996
